coredata.dc:title: Utilizing stress release effect for achieving high-performance photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution on CQDs-enriched SnSe nanosheets
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Yunhong
Zhai, Shuo
Ding, Yanhuai
Shen, Suling
Chen, Bin
Xie, Heping
coredata.dc:description: The effective development of solar hydrogen production technology is one of the most efficient ways to avoid secondary environmental pollution. However, the photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency of traditional semiconductor materials is too low, which is far from being able to alleviate the energy crisis and address related environmental issues. Here, we introduced SnSe, known for its high absorption coefficient in solar cell technology, as the main catalyst, and proposed a novel stress release effect strategy to overcome the bottleneck in photocatalytic hydrogen production. As a result, we have successfully released the interlayer stress of SnSe and introduced uniformly distributed carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on its surface. The experimental tests demonstrate that the release of interlayer stress and the introduction of CQDs not only enhance the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity, achieving twice the performance of commercial P25, but also significantly improve the separation efficiency of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, with a carrier density reaching 2.38 × 1031 cm−3. Compared to unmodified SnSe, the modified SnSe exhibits significantly enhanced photoelectrocatalytic performance, with a hydrogen evolution efficiency reaching 6.99 mmol∙h−1∙cm−2. This study demonstrates a novel stress release strategy for developing high-performance photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SnSe nanosheets
Stress release
Carbon quantum dots
Photocatalysis
Photoelectrocatalysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China Project [No. 51827901 , 62205212 ]; Program for Guang Dong Introducing Innovative and Entrepreneurial Teams [No. 2019ZT08G315 ]; Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Committee Foundation (No. KCXST20221021111601003); General program of China Postdoctoral Science Foundation [No. 2022\u202F M722179 ] and the Shenzhen Basic Research Program (No. JCYJ20210324093008021). The authors would also like to express their gratitude to their anonymous colleagues for their kind efforts and valuable comments for improving this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD spectras of SnSe related samples; (b) Raman spectras of the FL-SnSe and BL-SnSe (40–175 cm−1); (c) SAXS patterns of the samples; (d) ESR spectra of photocatalysts.

(a), (b) and (c) SEM image of the BL-SnSe; (d), (e) and (f) SEM image of the FL-SnSe; AFM image of the BL-SnSe (g) and the FL-SnSe (h); The thicknesses of the BL-SnSe and the FL-SnSe obtained at the red lines in Figures (g) and (h), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) and (b) TEM image of the BL-SnSe; (c) and (d) TEM image of the FL-SnSe; (e) Low-resolution TEM image of the FL-SnSe; (f) HRTEM image of the FL-SnSe; (g) TEM image of the analyzed area; Top-view TEM-EDX elemental mapping images showing a TEM image (h), Sn distribution (i), Se distribution (j), and C distribution (k).
High-resolution XPS spectra of SnSe for Sn 3d (a), Se 3d (b), and C 1 s (c)；High-resolution XPS spectra of FL-SnSe for Sn 3d (d), Se 3d (e), and C 1 s (f).

(a) Photoluminescence spectra of the samples; (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the samples; (c) (αhV)2 vs. hV for the samples; (d) (αhV)1/2 vs. hV for the samples; (e) Mott-Schottky plots of the samples; (f) Mott–Schottky plots of FL-SnSe.

LSV curves of the photoelectrodes under (a) dark condition and (b) light condition; (c) LSV curves of FL-SnSe photoelectrode under the dark condition and light condition; (d) Transient photocurrent responses of photoelectrodes without bias voltage; Nyquist plots showing the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) for the samples photoelectrode under (e) AM 1.5G illumination and (f) dark.

(a) Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution curves for the samples; (b) Recycling test of hydrogen evolution of the FL-SnSe under simulated sunlight; Photoelectrochemistry hydrogen evolution curves for the samples photoelectrode under (c) simulated sunlight and (d) dark.
Preparation of the few-layer SnSe nanosheets uniformly loaded with CQDs.
Principle of operation of a photoelectrochemical cell based on the FL-SnSe.

head.ce:title: Utilizing stress release effect for achieving high-performance photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution on CQDs-enriched SnSe nanosheets

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effective development of solar hydrogen production technology is one of the most efficient ways to avoid secondary environmental pollution. However, the photocatalytic hydrogen production efficiency of traditional semiconductor materials is too low, which is far from being able to alleviate the energy crisis and address related environmental issues. Here, we introduced SnSe, known for its high absorption coefficient in solar cell technology, as the main catalyst, and proposed a novel stress release effect strategy to overcome the bottleneck in photocatalytic hydrogen production. As a result, we have successfully released the interlayer stress of SnSe and introduced uniformly distributed carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on its surface. The experimental tests demonstrate that the release of interlayer stress and the introduction of CQDs not only enhance the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity, achieving twice the performance of commercial P25, but also significantly improve the separation efficiency of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, with a carrier density reaching 2.38 × 1031 cm−3. Compared to unmodified SnSe, the modified SnSe exhibits significantly enhanced photoelectrocatalytic performance, with a hydrogen evolution efficiency reaching 6.99 mmol∙h−1∙cm−2. This study demonstrates a novel stress release strategy for developing high-performance photoelectrochemical hydrogen evolution.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, in the face of rapidly depleting non-renewable energy sources, there is an increasing demand for new renewable energy to alleviate the energy crisis and address associated environmental issues such as global warming, air pollution, and marine and land pollution [1–5]. Exploring renewable and environmentally friendly energy sources has become a major global concern. Common types of new energy sources include solar energy [6,7], hydrogen energy [8,9], and biomass energy [10], among others. However, due to various factors such as natural conditions, economic conditions, and technological limitations, their widespread application has not been achieved. Among these renewable clean energy sources, hydrogen energy has the potential to replace traditional fossil fuels and other non-renewable energy sources due to its abundant storage, wide availability, and high energy density [5,11,12]. Water splitting, as a novel hydrogen production technology, has attracted significant attention in the academic field due to its advantages of low energy consumption, high efficiency, and environmental friendliness. Utilizing photocatalytic water splitting for hydrogen production is a crucial component in effectively addressing energy scarcity and environmental pollution issues. However, at present, the efficiency of solar energy conversion into hydrogen falls far below the level necessary for commercialization [13]. Therefore, selecting a suitable semiconductor material as the main component for photocatalysts becomes particularly important.

Photocatalysis is a process whereby semiconductors absorb photons and generate photoexcited electron-hole pairs. Some of the electrons and holes migrate to the surface of the material, where they engage in oxidation or reduction reactions with surface species, while others recombine, producing heat or luminescence. Among the mainstream photocatalytic materials, TiO2 [14], ZnO [15], CdS [16], Fe2O3 [17], In2O3 [18], and CuO [19] have been widely used as the main materials for photocatalysis. However, their narrow absorption range in the visible light region and relatively low absorption coefficients hinder the full utilization of their photocatalytic capabilities [13]. For instance, pure TiO2, pure In2O3, and pure ZnO exhibit excellent light absorption characteristics only in the ultraviolet region, while their abilities to absorb light in the visible and near-infrared regions are weak [13,20–22]. Although CdS, Fe2O3, and CuO demonstrate certain light absorption abilities in the visible region, their absorption ranges are still limited, hindering their commercial applications for promoting photocatalytic water splitting [13,23–25]. Even though modifications such as doping, altering crystal structures, or constructing p-n junctions can enhance their light absorption capabilities in the visible and near-infrared regions, their absorption coefficients still remain relatively low [26]. Therefore, it is crucial to explore materials with excellent light absorption properties for photocatalytic water splitting. SnSe exhibits an appropriate bandgap, excellent optoelectronic properties, and a relatively high absorption coefficient, making it increasingly applied as an absorber material in solar cells [27–31]. However, when used as the main component in photocatalysts, SnSe has not fully demonstrated photocatalytic activity, and the recombination rate of photoexcited electrons is rapid [27,32,33]. If the high absorption coefficient of SnSe can be effectively utilized and the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs can be minimized to the maximum extent, it has the potential to compete with other mainstream photocatalytic materials and even exhibit superior photocatalytic activity. This is attributed to its significantly higher capability in generating photoexcited electron-hole pairs compared to other mainstream photocatalytic materials [30,34–36]. To minimize the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs to the greatest extent, it may be possible to improve the photocatalytic activity of the semiconductor by releasing interlayer stress to create more active sites.

SnSe has a high interlayer binding energy, more than twice that of MoS2, making it difficult to release interlayer stress through simple exfoliation methods [37]. This study employed an improved lithium ion embedding method and utilized a high-temperature, high-pressure-assisted liquid phase exfoliation process to prepare few-layer SnSe, thereby releasing interlayer stress. Compared to the SnSe nanosheets where interlayer stress was not released, these SnSe nanosheets demonstrated enhanced light absorption efficiency and improved availability of active sites, thereby enhancing their photocatalytic activity [29,32]. However, the interlayer stress release in SnSe nanosheets results in the generation of electron-hole pairs with high binding energy, hindering their natural separation and impeding the transfer of electrons and holes to the material surface for catalytic reactions, ultimately resulting in recombination and heat generation [38]. Therefore, in this study, after interlayer stress release, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were further introduced onto the surface of SnSe nanosheets to generate an internal potential driving the separation of electron-hole pairs, thereby enhancing its photocatalytic activity [39], achieving twice the performance of commercial P25 (a commercially available titanium dioxide material consisting of a mixture of anatase and rutile phases with an average particle size of 25 nm), and with a photoelectrocatalytic hydrogen evolution efficiency reaching 6.99 mmol∙h−1∙cm−2. This research provides an alternative approach to the selection of photocatalysts, focusing on choosing materials with high absorption coefficients that match solar radiation as the primary photocatalyst, rather than solely modifying mainstream photocatalysts based on their light absorption properties. Additionally, the enhancement in catalytic activity is explained from the perspective of interlayer stress release.

The fabrication process of ultrathin SnSe nanosheets uniformly loaded with CQDs is shown in Scheme 1 . Typically, 100 mg of Se powder was dissolved in 100 ml of 10 mol/L NaOH solution under vigorous stirring to form a reddish-brown solution A. Solution B was formed by dissolving 20 g of citric acid (CA) and 0.9 g SnCl2 2H2O in 100 ml of deionized water with gentle stirring. Then, the solution B was slowly dropped into the solution A under vigorous stirring. The color of the solution A gradually became lighter at the beginning, but a gray precipitate could be obtained with the further dropwise addition of the solution. Then, the solution with gray precipitate was transferred to the oil bath solution at 200 °C and kept for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was washed with deionized water, filtered, and dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h and vacuum annealed at 200 °C for 2 h to obtain bulk SnSe nanosheets, labeled as BL-SnSe. If AC is not added during the process, the obtained bulk SnSe nanosheets are labeled as SnSe.

Dissolve 1.28 g LiOH and 10 g CA in 60 mL of ethylene glycol (EG) to form a clear solution, then add 0.66 g of the prepared BL-SnSe to the above clear solution with constant stirring, and transfer it to a 100 ml microminiature the reactor was kept at 200 °C and a stirring rate of 300 r/min for about 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solid powder in the solution can be collected by filtration and washed with acetone and ethanol to remove the remaining EG solution. Then, the precipitate was washed with deionized water and filtered, vacuum dried at 60 °C for 12 h, and then vacuum annealed at 200 °C for 2 h to obtain few-layer SnSe nanosheets, labeled as FL-SnSe. If AC is not added during this process, and BL-SnSe is replaced with SnSe, the resulting few-layer SnSe nanosheets are labeled as F-SnSe.

SnSe is a promising semiconductor material that has attracted significant attention in the field of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. Firstly, we employed X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique to analyze the crystal structures of SnSe, BL-SnSe, FL-SnSe and F-SnSe. As shown in Fig. 1
a, distinct diffraction peaks were observed at specific angles, indicating the presence of well-defined crystal planes, with all the peaks precisely pointing towards the orthorhombic SnSe phase (JCPDS: 48–1224). Upon CQD modification, significant carbon peaks emerged, and the intensities of some characteristic peaks reduced, suggesting a structural modification induced by CQDs. When analyzing the XRD patterns of FL-SnSe, the appearance of new peaks and noticeable attenuation of characteristic peak intensities were observed. These changes indicate that the reduction of snse crystal size and the interaction with CQD cause lattice strain, defect formation, and even phase transition. Due to the low carbon content, the diffraction peaks associated with carbon in the XRD spectra were weak, which hindered the analysis of the interaction between CQDs and SnSe. Therefore, Raman spectroscopy was employed to further investigate the composition of the samples. As shown in Figure S2 a, no strong carbon signals were detected in the Raman spectrum of SnSe within the range of 1000 cm−1 to 2000 cm−1. However, in the case of FL-SnSe, two strong and broad peaks corresponding to the D and G bands of CQDs were observed, confirming the presence of CQDs in FL-SnSe. The G band in the spectrum shifted to a slightly higher wavenumber compared to pristine graphite (1581 cm−1), indicating a strong interaction between CQDs and SnSe, leading to the formation of some defects during the interaction process. The release of interlayer stress in SnSe was further characterized using Raman spectroscopy [40–42]. As shown in Fig. 1 b and
Figure S2
b, we obtained the Raman characteristic peaks of four samples at 40–175 cm−1. The shifts in the peak positions indicate the release of interlayer stress. Both FL-SnSe and F-SnSe exhibit noticeable shifts in characteristic peaks relative to SnSe, while BL-SnSe shows no significant shift, suggesting the release of interlayer stress in SnSe after size reduction. In order to further analyze the crystalline state of SnSe samples and the changes after loading carbon quantum dots, small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) tests were conducted at the Beijing Synchrotron Radiation Station. As shown in Fig. 1 c, SnSe and F-SnSe exhibit only a single characteristic peak, while BL-SnSe and FL-SnSe show two characteristic peaks, indicating the successful introduction of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with a hexagonal graphite structure. This further confirms the incorporation of CQDs. Additionally, it is observed that both the thinning of thickness and the introduction of CQDs cause slight shifts in the peak positions. The shift caused by thinning can be attributed to the release of interlayer stress in SnSe, while the shift induced by CQDs can be attributed to the interaction between CQDs and SnSe. The close contact between CQDs and SnSe, as well as the release of interlayer stress, are beneficial for the separation of electron-hole pairs, thereby promoting the hydrogen evolution reaction [43–45]. The carbon content in FL-SnSe and BL-SnSe was investigated using thermal gravimetric analysis and differential scanning calorimetry (TG-DSC) ( Figure S4 ). The slight weight loss below 350 °C can be attributed to the evaporation of adsorbed water. The second weight loss observed around 500 °C is attributed to the oxidation of carbon. Subsequently, the weight loss between 550 °C and 600 °C may be related to the oxidation of SnSe. The weight percentages of SnSe in FL-SnSe and BL-SnSe were calculated to be approximately 61.8 % and 85.5 %, respectively. The corresponding weight percentages of CQDs were estimated to be approximately 29.0 % and 5.3 % in FL-SnSe and BL-SnSe, respectively. Figure S5 show the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and corresponding BJH pore size distribution curves of SnSe, BL-SnSe, FL-SnSe and F-SnSe, respectively. The observed isotherms in the figures can be identified as type IV, exhibiting H1 hysteresis loop as defined by IUPAC, indicating the mesoporous features of the samples [46]. Compared to bulk SnSe, few-layer SnSe exhibits a broader pore size distribution and a larger specific surface area, with an increased presence of mesopores. These mesopores can facilitate the formation of more active sites in solution and promote the hydrogen evolution reaction. In order to investigate the relationship between defects (Se vacancies) in the prepared composite materials and their photocatalytic performance, electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR) was employed to characterize the electronic properties of the materials. As shown in Fig. 1 d, the test results for SnSe did not observe any ESR signals at g = 2.003, while a small ESR signal was observed for F-SnSe, indicating the formation of some Se vacancy defects during stress release. FL-SnSe and BL-SnSe exhibited significant ESR signals (with FL-SnSe showing a stronger ESR signal), suggesting that the strong interaction between CQDs and SnSe further promotes the formation of Se vacancy defects, which are conducive to the formation of active sites for electron capture [7,47]. Furthermore, the high concentration of active sites in FL-SnSe is favorable for the light-induced generation of active intermediates, which is an ideal characteristic for enhancing photocatalytic performance. Therefore, the observed ESR results predict that FL-SnSe possesses excellent photocatalytic performance.

Fig. 2 depicts the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of BL-SnSe (Fig. 2a, 2b, and 2c) and FL-SnSe (Fig. 2d, 2e, and 2f). As shown in Fig. 2a, 2b, and 2c, BL-SnSe consists of randomly stacked nanosheets with uniform thickness. The nanosheets exhibit relatively smooth and uniform surfaces, and the constituent crystalline particles appear to be densely packed without noticeable irregularities or defects at the microscopic level. This indicates the presence of a well-ordered crystalline structure in BL-SnSe. In contrast, the SEM images of FL-SnSe demonstrate distinct surface morphologies, and FL-SnSe has a thinner thickness because the reduction in thickness introduces surface effects that lead to structural distortions. Additionally, the incorporation of CQDs results in a combination of increased surface roughness and the presence of dispersed small dots on the surface, as observed in the surface morphology. This suggests successful deposition of CQDs. These CQDs can function as localized defect sites or dopants, thereby enhancing the electronic and optical properties of the material. The AFM results provide important insights into the surface characteristics and topography of BL-SnSe and FL-SnSe samples upon the introduction of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2g, and 2 h, the AFM results demonstrate that the introduction of CQDs on the surfaces of BL-SnSe and FL-SnSe leads to noticeable surface modifications, with increased roughness and the presence of dispersed CQDs. In Fig. 2i, it is evident that the thickness of BL-SnSe and FL-SnSe is approximately 80 nm and 5 nm, respectively, providing further evidence for the successful preparation of few-layer SnSe and the release of interlayer stress.

The TEM images in Fig. 3
a and 3b indicate that the TEM image of BL-SnSe exhibits a relatively thick, plate-like structure with a higher number of atomic layers, making it difficult to observe clear lattice fringes. However, it demonstrates a relatively large grain size, and the lattice planes are visible, indicating a high degree of crystallinity. Conversely, the observed FL-SnSe in Fig. 3 c and 3d shows a different morphology. These images illustrate thin nanosheet structures with a limited number of atomic layers, and a significant amount of uniformly dispersed CQDs are observed on the surface, indicating their successful introduction onto the SnSe surface. Furthermore, a higher magnification TEM image in Fig. 3 e reveals the specific morphology and distribution of the CQDs. Some CQDs partially aggregate to form nano-clusters distributed on the SnSe surface, which not only increases the specific surface area of FL-SnSe but also facilitates the hydrogen evolution reaction. HRTEM images show lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.336 nm and 0.24 nm (Fig. 3 f), matching the (210) plane of SnSe and the (111) plane of graphite, respectively. Detailed elemental distribution of the FL-SnSe surface can be observed from the top view TEM-EDX mappings (Fig. 3 g-k), once again confirming the successful introduction of CQDs onto the SnSe surface. The lateral size distributions of CQDs particles and CQDs clusters were determined, and as shown in Figure S8 , the average sizes of CQDs particles and CQDs clusters were calculated to be 3.67 nm and 16.33 nm, respectively. Such small sizes may be the reason for the appearance of amorphous carbon peaks in XRD.

High-resolution XPS was further conducted to investigate the interaction between SnSe nanosheets and CQDs in the FL-SnSe nanocomposite. The measured spectra of the FL-SnSe nanocomposite (
Figure S9 ) confirmed the presence of C, Sn, and Se. Furthermore, no impurity elements other than those inherent to the material itself were detected, indicating the successful preparation of each photocatalyst. To further characterize the successful preparation of the photocatalysts, detailed spectra of Sn 3d, Se 3d, and C 1 s were examined for both FL-SnSe and SnSe. The high-resolution Sn 3d XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. 4
a and 4d, confirm the presence of two main peaks at 487.1 eV and 495.5 eV in the high-resolution Sn 3d XPS spectrum of SnSe, corresponding to the Sn 3d5/2 and Sn 3d3/2 peaks of Sn2+, respectively. The peaks at 485.4 eV and 493.9 eV can be attributed to the Sn-Se bond. In the high-resolution Sn 3d XPS spectrum of FL-SnSe, the two main peaks show no significant shift, indicating that the state of Sn element is not affected by stress release and carbon loading. However, it is worth noting that there is a slight shift in the positions of two small peaks, which can be attributed to the influence of stress release and carbon loading on the state of Sn-Se bond [48]. The high-resolution Se 3d XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. 4 b and 4e, reveal that in the high-resolution Se 3d XPS spectrum of SnSe, the peaks at 54.6 eV and 55.9 eV are attributed to the binding energy of Se 3d in SnSe. After stress release and carbon loading, these two peaks show partial shifts, indicating a strong interaction between Se and C in the FL-SnSe. This is further confirmed by the appearance of a peak at 53.5 eV in the high-resolution Sn 3d XPS spectrum of FL-SnSe [48]. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. 4 c and 4f, exhibit four binding energy peaks at 282.50 eV, 284.80 eV, 286.4 eV, and 288.9 eV, corresponding to sp2 hybridized carbon–carbon double bond (C = C), sp hybridized carbon–carbon triple bond (C≡C), carbon–oxygen single bond (C-O), and carbon–oxygen double bond (C = O), respectively [49,50]. Compared to SnSe, the peaks associated with FL-SnSe show varying degrees of shifts. Additionally, there is an additional small peak at 281.9 eV, which corresponds to the peak at 53.5 eV in the high-resolution Se 3d XPS spectrum, indicating a strong interaction between Se and C. These results suggest that the chemical interaction and strong bonding between SnSe nanosheets and CQDs are manifested as Se-C chemical bonds. This bond not only serves as a connector for charge transfer but also maintains the stable structure of the FL-SnSe nanocomposite, thereby enhancing the hydrogen evolution performance of the composite material.

The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectroscopy has been employed to elucidate the efficiency of carrier capture, transfer, and separation in semiconductors. The effective separation of photo-generated holes and electrons plays a crucial role in the photocatalytic performance of materials. It is generally believed that a stronger PL peak indicates a higher recombination rate of electron-hole pairs and lower separation efficiency. Fig. 5
a shows the PL spectra of the samples measured at room temperature using an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. The peaks between 350 nm and 550 nm can be attributed to the recombination of electron-hole pairs in the samples. It is evident that among all the tested samples, P25 exhibits the lowest signal intensity in the visible light region, while F-SnSe shows the highest signal intensity, indicating the highest recombination rate of electron-hole pairs in F-SnSe. Therefore, it is confirmed that the few-layer SnSe nanosheets generate electron-hole pairs with high binding energy, leading to a lower efficiency in the separation of electron-hole pairs. However, with the introduction of CQDs, it is clearly observed that the recombination efficiency of electron-hole pairs in BL-SnSe decreases. Furthermore, after thinning the SnSe nanoplates with introduced CQDs, the recombination efficiency of electron-hole pairs in FL-SnSe further decreases, but there is still a significant gap compared to P25, suggesting room for further improvement. Fig. 5 b illustrates the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the prepared photocatalysts. Compared to P25, SnSe clearly exhibits a broader absorption range and higher absorption intensity in the visible light region. Although SnSe has weaker absorption intensity in the ultraviolet region, considering the energy contribution, SnSe still absorbs a significantly greater amount of solar radiation than P25. Furthermore, the introduction of CQDs and thinning of the bulk material only results in slight changes in wavelengths above 500 nm, indicating no significant enhancement in overall solar absorption. Therefore, it can be concluded that SnSe and its modified materials have a stronger sunlight absorption capacity compared to P25. The band gap (Eg) of the photocatalysts was obtained by fitting the UV–Vis absorption spectra using the Tauc/Davis-Mott model. As shown in Fig. 5 c and 5d, compared to P25, SnSe exhibits lower values for both the direct and indirect band gaps, indicating a smaller intrinsic band gap in SnSe. This significantly increases the probability of recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs and hinders the full utilization of solar radiation. However, when SnSe is modified with CQDs and interlayer stress release, it exhibits an improved direct bandgap, while the indirect bandgap remains unchanged. This indicates that the modification with CQDs and interlayer stress release expands the direct bandgap of SnSe, which is beneficial for suppressing the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs and enhancing the quantum efficiency.

As shown in Fig. 5 e, the Mott-Schottky plot displays a positive slope for P25, indicating it as an n-type semiconductor, while SnSe and its modified materials exhibit a negative slope, indicating them as p-type semiconductors. The carrier density of a material is related to the slope of the Mott-Schottky plot. The carrier density can be calculated using the following formula based on the slope:
N d
=
2
e 0
ε ε
0
Â ·
d
C
- 2
dV
- 1

where e0 is the electron charge of 1.602 × 10-19C, ε is the relative permittivity of 31 (for anatase), ε0 is the vacuum permittivity of 8.854 × 10-12F∙m−1, Nd is the carrier density and d(C-2)/dV is the straight slope. By calculation, it is found that among all the tested samples, FL-SnSe has the highest carrier density (2.38 × 1031 cm−3), while SnSe has the lowest carrier density (2.41 × 1030 cm−3). This indicates that the modification with CQDs and interlayer stress release is beneficial for enhancing the carrier density and promoting the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. The valence band position (EVB) and conduction band position (ECB) of FL-SnSe can be determined through Mott-Schottky calculations. Firstly, based on the plot of (αhV)2 against hV, the band gap (Eg) of FL-SnSe was determined to be 1.74 eV. As shown in Fig. 7, the negative slope observed in the Mott-Schottky measurements of FL-SnSe curves at different frequencies confirms its p-type semiconductor behavior, and the intercept reveals a flat band potential (EFB) at 0.84 eV (Fig. 5 f). Considering that for p-type semiconductors, EVB is nearly equal to EFB, differing by approximately 0.1–0.3 eV, the estimated EVB of FL-SnSe is around 1.04 eV. By using the formula (Eg = EVB − ECB), the estimated ECB of FL-SnSe is approximately −0.7 eV.

In order to evaluate the HER performance of catalysts (in 2.5 M Na2SO3), LSV scanning of the catalysts was conducted in a three-electrode PEC cell system under dark and light (AM1.5) conditions. Fig. 6
a and 6b illustrate the current density curves of SnSe, F-SnSe, BL-SnSe, and FL-SnSe under dark and bright conditions, respectively. In the dark reaction, the FL-SnSe photoelectrode exhibits a lower overpotential (approximately −0.5 V) compared to other photoelectrodes. At a potential of −0.8 V, the FL-SnSe photoelectrode shows a much higher current density of 5.59 mV/cm−2 (vs. RHE) compared to other photoelectrodes under the same conditions. When illuminated with an AM1.5 light source, the overpotential of all the photoelectrodes decreases (Fig. 6 c and Figures S10
a and S10b). The overpotential of the FL-SnSe photoelectrode decreases from −0.5 V to −0.40 V. The FL-SnSe photoelectrode exhibits a current density of 11.58 mV/cm2 (vs. RHE) at a potential of −0.8 V, whereas the BL-SnSe, F-SnSe and SnSe photoelectrodes only have 7.96 mV/cm2, 6.72 mV/cm2 and 5.29 mV/cm2, respectively. Therefore, in both photocatalytic and photoelectron-catalytic reactions, the FL-SnSe photoelectrode exhibits the highest hydrogen evolution activity among the four photoelectrodes. Fig. 6 d illustrates the TPC response of the photoelectrodes without bias voltage (with multiple 20 s on/off cycles) under AM1.5 illumination. Compared to other samples, the FL-SnSe photoelectrode exhibits a higher photocurrent response. In the absence of light, the photocurrent density is nearly zero, and it quickly recovers to a steady-state value upon illumination, which can be repeated in multiple on/off cycles. It is worth noting that under continuous illumination, the photocurrent density hardly decreases ( Figure S11 ). Compared to the other two photoelectrodes, the FL-SnSe photoelectrode exhibits a higher photocurrent response, indicating the generation of a significant number of photoinduced charge carriers. It is worth noting that, compared to SnSe, although F-SnSe exhibits a higher recombination rate of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, the photoelectrodes prepared from F-SnSe generate a larger number of charge carriers than those prepared from SnSe. This indicates that, on the basis of promoting the separation of electron-hole pairs, the photocatalytic performance of semiconductor materials with a high light absorption coefficient can be more effectively improved. The semicircle radius on the EIS spectrum reflects the interfacial layer resistance of the electrode surface, and the arc radius is inversely proportional to the charge transfer efficiency. As shown in Fig. 6 e and 6f, under AM 1.5G illumination and dark conditions, the interface resistance of the FL-SnSe photoelectrode is significantly lower compared to the other photoelectrodes. Moreover, the interface resistance under dark conditions is much higher than that under illumination. The FL-SnSe photoelectrode exhibits a higher charge transfer efficiency under illumination conditions, which is beneficial for the hydrogen evolution reaction. The above results indicate that the introduction of CQDs and the release of interlayer stress contribute to the enhancement of the photoelectrocatalytic activity of SnSe.

The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of the sample powder under simulated sunlight is shown in Fig. 7
a. FL-SnSe exhibits the best hydrogen evolution performance among all the samples, reaching 2.96 mmol∙h−1∙g−1, which is twice that of the commercial P25 (1.43 mmol∙h−1∙g−1). The slight performance decrease within 4 h is attributed to detrimental reactions caused by the consumption of hydrogen or sacrificial agents in the testing system. Fig. 7 b displays the repeated photocatalytic hydrogen evolution test of FL-SnSe photocatalyst under AM 1.5G irradiation, showing excellent durability with no significant drop in activity. Even after the fifth cycle (20 h), the hydrogen production efficiency reached 89.5 % of the first hour. The XRD, SEM, and TEM images of FL-SnSe after 20 h of cycling tests are shown in Fig. 7 c, Figure S12, and S13, respectively. It is evident from the XRD, SEM, and TEM results of FL-SnSe after 20 h of cycling tests that, following 5 repeated photocatalytic hydrogen evolution tests, there were no significant changes in the morphology or structure of the photocatalyst, providing sufficient evidence for its good stability. The results indicate that the FL-SnSe photocatalyst still maintains good stability under illumination. The turnover number (TON) reached ∼ 66.829 in 4 h with a turnover frequency (TOF) of 16.707 for FL-SnSe. The TON and TOF of other samples are listed in Table 1, indicating that the FL-SnSe has the best hydrogen evolution ability in the photocatalytic reaction system. As shown in Fig. 7 d, compared to other advanced catalysts, FL-SnSe still remains highly competitive [51–64]. As shown in Fig. 7 e and 7f, under simulated sunlight, the hydrogen evolution rate of each electrode is significantly higher compared to that in the dark due to the synergistic effect of light and electricity, which promotes charge separation and transfer on the catalysts. Additionally, the hydrogen evolution rates of each electrode remain nearly unchanged within 4 h, even after 10 h, there was little to no change in the photocurrent density, demonstrating excellent stability. Compared to other electrodes, the FL-SnSe electrode exhibits the best hydrogen evolution efficiency under simulated sunlight (6.99 mmol∙h−1∙cm−2) and dark conditions (3.26 mmol∙h−1∙cm−2), indicating its superior charge separation and transfer ability. The above results fully demonstrate that the uniform distribution of CQDs and the release of interlayer stress in SnSe are beneficial for improving the performance of SnSe catalysts, providing insights for the industrial development of hydrogen evolution catalysts. As shown in Table 1, the TON and TOF of FL-SnSe and BL-SnSe are not significantly different. Furthermore, the other samples in the study do not contain CQDs. Therefore, it is not possible to directly assess the photoelectrochemical performance of the prepared samples based solely on their TON and TOF values.

To further understand the reaction mechanism in the aforementioned photo/photoelectro-catalytic reactions, a brief description is presented in Scheme 2 . The composite material exposed to sunlight mainly generates photoinduced electron-hole pairs (excitons) from SnSe. In the absence of interlayer stress release, the transfer of electrons between layers is hindered, with most of them being confined to intralayer propagation. However, this propagation path is relatively long, resulting in the majority of electrons and holes failing to reach the active sites for participation in redox reactions before recombination occurs, thereby affecting catalytic activity. If interlayer stress can be released and CQDs introduced on the surface, the combination of Sn atoms on the surface of SnSe with CQDs will lead to the reorganization of defective surface positions into a structure more conducive to electron accumulation. Consequently, photoinduced electrons can rapidly transfer from the conduction band to CQDs through interfacial interactions, generating an internal electric field within the composite material and providing active sites for the reduction of H+ ions, thereby obtaining electrons for the conversion into H2. In addition, TEOA near the few-layered SnSe reacts with holes to generate TEOA+. If the composite material is used as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and platinum mesh as the counter electrode, under the driving force of applied voltage, electron transfer becomes more rapid. Subsequently, electrons transfer to the counter electrode through CQDs and the three-electrode circuit. H+ near the counter electrode is converted into H2 by gaining electrons. Additionally, SO3 2- near the working electrode reacts with holes to generate SO4 2-. Therefore, we believe that FL-SnSe can effectively separate photoinduced electron-hole pairs and delay their recombination, further promoting the progress of the hydrogen evolution reaction.

In summary, we have reported a photo/photoelectrocatalyst based on SnSe nanosheets with interlayer stress release and uniformly loaded CQDs. The catalyst is prepared through an improved lithium ion intercalation method and in-situ deposition of graphite carbon. The catalyst powder exhibits enhanced hydrogen evolution performance under simulated sunlight, approximately twice that of commercial P25. When employed as a photoelectrode, this catalyst demonstrates photocurrent activity similar to platinum for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), with a carrier density reaching 2.38 × 1031 cm−3. At an external bias of 0.8 V, a current density of 11.58 mA/cm2 is achieved. Moreover, it exhibits extremely high stability. It is noteworthy that the catalytic performance is poor when pure SnSe is used as a photo/photoelectro-catalyst. However, significant improvement in the catalytic activity can be achieved by modifying it based on its high absorption coefficient to enhance the effective separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. The modified SnSe nanosheets reported in this study not only provide a new strategy for selecting photo/photoelectrocatalysts as the main component, but also offer a new pathway for the modification of nanomaterials from the perspective of stress release. According to this strategy, it is possible to design more efficient and sustainable photoelectrodes, providing a reference for further advancement in solar-driven water splitting for hydrogen production technology.

Yunhong Jiang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Shuo Zhai: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Yanhuai Ding: . Suling Shen: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Bin Chen: . Heping Xie: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

The authors would also like to express their gratitude to their anonymous colleagues for their kind efforts and valuable comments for improving this work.
